elements.sent-2411-001: We study in detail the postselection problem in a specific model: bosons hopping on a lattice subjected to continuous local measurements of quadrature observables.
elements.sent-2411-002: We solve the model analytically and show that the postselection overhead can be reduced by postprocessing the entire measurement record into one or two numbers for each trajectory and then postselecting based only on these numbers.
elements.sent-2411-003: We then provide a step-by-step protocol designed to recover connected two-point functions of the quantum trajectories, which display an exponentially decaying profile that is not observable in the unconditional, trajectory averaged, state.
elements.sent-2411-004: With the analytical solution in hand, we analyse the features of this postprocessing stage with the intention of abstracting away the properties that make postselection feasible in this model and may help in mitigating postselection in more general settings.
elements.sent-2411-005: We also test the protocol numerically in a way that utilizes only experimentally accessible information, showing that various quantum trajectory observables can be recovered with a few repetitions of the numerical experiment, even after including inevitable coarse-graining procedures expected under realistic experimental conditions.
elements.sent-2411-006: Furthermore, all the information required to design the postprocessing stage is independently present both in the unconditional dynamics and also in the measurement record, thus bypassing the need to solve for the conditional evolution of the model.
elements.sent-2411-007: We finalize by providing experimental implementations of these models in cavity-QED and circuit-QED.
elements.sent-2411-008: Measurements in quantum mechanics are like a bargain with the devil: in exchange for a reduced amount of information, we perturb the measured physical system in a fundamentally unavoidable, often destructive, way.
elements.sent-2411-009: Nevertheless, carefully designed measurement strategies can exploit the little information we gain to then prepare entangled/non-classical quantum states by means of e.g. quantum non-demolition approaches~\cite{Braginsky1996,Kuzmich2000,SchleierSmith2010,Cox2016,Hosten2016 or heralding~\cite{Knill2001,Nielsen2004,Moehring2007,Cao2024 , making measurements an important resource for modern quantum technologies.}}
elements.sent-2411-010: This is a capability that has been long recognized in the study of monitored quantum systems~\cite{belavkin2005,Bergquist1986,Korotkov1999,Jacobs2006,Carmichael2008,Wiseman_Milburn_2009,Clerk2010,Minev2019 , and over the years many works have focused on tractable few-body models for the purposes of estimation and sensing tasks~\cite{Caves1980,Mabuchi_1996,Tsang2012,Gammelmark2014 .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Quantum mechanical measurements can also give rise to unexpected phenomena in many-body dynamics~\cite{Li2018,Chan2019,Skinner2019,annurevFisher .}
elements.sent-2411-012: In this different context, the competition between local projective measurements and random unitary gates was found to lead to a transition in the entanglement structure of the system under study, from an area law phase where measurements dominate, to a volume law phase where unitary evolution efficiently scrambles information.
elements.sent-2411-013: Since this discovery, many efforts have been devoted to elucidate the role played by measurements in the many-body setting~\cite{Gullans2020,Bao2020,Ippoliti2021,Alberton2021,Minoguchi2022,Poboiko2024 and how they fit into our understanding of quantum phases of matter~\cite{Friedman2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: In particular, there has been a lot of emphasis on characterization by means of quantum information probes such as von-Neumann and Renyi entropies, and mutual informations, which provide basis-independent diagnostics of entanglement and correlations.
elements.sent-2411-015: At the same time, many-body systems undergoing measurement dynamics suffer from the so-called ``postselection problem".
elements.sent-2411-016: This problem is the consequence of two facts: (1) the quantum state $\ket{\psi}$ of a system that has been subject to measurement+unitary dynamics depends (in principle) on the outcomes of all the measurements that were performed during its evolution history; (2) to be able to extract information from $\ket{\psi}$ by means of statistics (to obtain, e.g., the variance of an observable) one needs to build an ensemble of identical copies of $\ket{\psi}$.
elements.sent-2411-017: Characterizing $\ket{\psi}$ thus requires repeating the same measurement+unitary dynamics with the same outcomes for all the measurements.
elements.sent-2411-018: Since the result of a quantum mechanical measurement is non-deterministic, in most cases the outcomes will not be the same.
elements.sent-2411-019: In fact, for extended systems and evolutions with an extensive number of measurements, the probability of obtaining the same set of outcomes is exponentially small in system size and evolution time.
elements.sent-2411-020: Thus, building an ensemble of copies of $\ket{\psi}$ (i.e. the ``postselected ensemble") becomes impossible as a matter of principle, except for very small systems.
elements.sent-2411-021: Because of this, experimental observations of measurement-induced transitions are challenging~\cite{Noel2022,Koh2023,Hoke2023 , even after overlooking the inherent complications of measuring entropies~\cite{Sackett2000,Kaufman2016,Kokail2021,Joshi2023 for large systems.}}
elements.sent-2411-022: To address this singular complication, which can put into question the identification of measurement-induced dynamical phases as genuine phases of matter~\cite{Friedman2023 , further research has focused on discerning circumstances in which the complexities introduced by postselection can be partially mitigated: circuits with space-time duality~\cite{Ippoliti2021b,Lu2021 , the use of quantum-classical hybrid approaches in cases where classical simulation is feasible~\cite{Li2023,McGinley2024,Garratt2024 , fully connected models where only a small subregion of Hilbert space is explored~\cite{Passarelli2024 , systems in the semiclassical regime~\cite{li2024,delmonte2024 , etc. In fact, the ability to overcome postselection in an efficient way may be an important characterization on its own, possibly related to the ability to use the measurement record to learn information of the quantum state that is subjected to the hybrid unitary+measurement dynamics~\cite{Dehghani2023,Akhtar2024,Ippoliti2024 .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Moreover, applying feedback operations conditioned on the measurement outcomes has been shown to lead to effects that can be observed without postselection~\cite{Ivanov2020,Ivanov2021,Friedman2023,Piroli2023,Ravindranath2023,wang2023,Odea2024,Hauser2024 , although the connection to the original evolution without feedback is tenuous because the extra applied operations strongly modify the dynamics of the system in question~\cite{Ravindranath2023,Piroli2023,Odea2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: In this paper we study the structure of postselection in an analytically solvable model that is also experimentally realizable using modern quantum platforms: bosons hopping on a lattice subjected to local continuous measurements of quadrature observables.
elements.sent-2411-025: Instead of focusing on entropies, we investigate local observables, for which the requirements on postselection are loosened drastically.
elements.sent-2411-026: This is because local observables in a given quantum trajectory are reconstructed by:.
elements.sent-2411-027: Distilling the macroscopic number of measurement results into a few numbers (one or two), called ``estimators"~\cite{Jacobs_2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Binning quantum trajectories together according to whether the estimators acquire the same value, as opposed to the entire measurement record.
elements.sent-2411-029: Importantly, the estimators depend on the choice of observable.
elements.sent-2411-030: In practice, the post-selection problem is still present because determining the relation between the estimators and the measurement record is generally a hard task, but in our model we can construct them explicitly.. We show that:.
elements.sent-2411-031: The estimators display structure consistent with spatial locality, in the following sense: if we want to reproduce two-point correlation functions nearby locations $\mathbf{r,r'}$ and at an observation time $T$, the relevant estimators are constructed from the measurement record in the vicinities of $\mathbf{r,r'}$ and close to the time $T$.
elements.sent-2411-032: This is a consequence of the emergence of a correlation length $\xi$ and an associated memory time $\tau$ as a result of the competition between measurements and unitary evolution~\cite{Gullans2020b,Minoguchi2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Unlike the case of random circuits, $\xi$ and $\tau$ are not statistical~\cite{Gullans2020b,Ippoliti2021 , but are properties of each trajectory.}
elements.sent-2411-034: In our lattice model we can tune the parameters to make the correlation length much larger than the lattice spacing, leading to an effective continuous description.
elements.sent-2411-035: The procedure to construct the estimators in terms of the measurement record can be inferred directly from the measurement record itself, or by an adequate analysis of the unconditional dynamics, i.e. after averaging over all possible measurement realizations~\cite{Muller2009 .}
elements.sent-2411-036: Although this is true in general, the problem of reconstructing the estimator is substantially simplified if there is some prior knowledge about the functional relation between the measurement record and the estimator.
elements.sent-2411-037: We also verify these results by performing numerical experiments that mimic actual experimental conditions, where after a single iteration of an experiment the only outcomes are the measurement record and a single copy of the quantum state.
elements.sent-2411-038: This procedure further illustrates that the conditional evolution needs to be repeated only a few times ($\sim 10^4$ is sufficient for our model), and that many different observables can be recovered using the same set of quantum trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-039: Quadratic bosonic models in the context of measurement transitions have been investigated in the past~\cite{Minoguchi2022,wang2023,yokomizo2024 , and fall within the scope of Kalman-Bucy filter theory~\cite{Kalman1960,BELAVKIN1999,Jacobs_2014 .}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Although they are analytically solvable, some of their phenomenology, such as the emergence of a memory time, extends beyond the setting of quadratic bosons~\cite{lin2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-041: This may very well provide some mitigation of postselection in some situations.
elements.sent-2411-042: We thus approach our model with the explicit intention of identifying features that may generalize beyond the quadratic boson realm.
elements.sent-2411-043: This paper is organized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-044: We introduce the lattice model and commment on its basic features.
elements.sent-2411-045: We study the system in a single lattice site.
elements.sent-2411-046: The solution in this simplified setting will guide the investigation of the full lattice system.
elements.sent-2411-047: Here we describe a step-by-step procedure to recover variances of observables.
elements.sent-2411-048: We also show that this procedure can be designed based only on information present in the measurement record and/or the unconditional dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-049: We embark on the analysis of the lattice system introduced in Section~\ref{sec:Def and provide fully analytical solutions for its stationary steady state.}
elements.sent-2411-050: We show that the postselection procedure introduced in Section~\ref{sec:SingleSite works in this setting too.}
elements.sent-2411-051: We also discuss some features of postselction as the continuum limit is approached.
elements.sent-2411-052: We describe possible experimental implementations of the models studied in the previous sections.

elements.subsection-2411-002: Designing the filter from experimentally accessible quantities

elements.sent-2411-325: In this paper we have studied the postselection problem in a system of bosons subjected to both unitary dynamics and continuous measurements.
elements.sent-2411-326: We first analyzed the case of a single site, which illustrates in a simple setting many important features of postselection, and then considered the extension to many sites to investigate the interplay between these features and the spatial structure of extended systems.
elements.sent-2411-327: More concretely, we showed that to recover observables that are nonlinear in the conditional density matrix it is not necessary to postselect quantum states based on the entire measurement history.
elements.sent-2411-328: Instead, it suffices to do postselection based on a few numbers, i.e. the ``estimators", which are calculated directly from the measurement record.
elements.sent-2411-329: We showed this both analytically and by performing numerical experiments that mimic actual experimental conditions, where experimenters only have access to the measurement record and to a single copy of the conditional quantum state.
elements.sent-2411-330: By studying the structure of the estimators, we identified the presence of a memory time, indicating that only the measurement record close to the observation time is relevant for the recovery of nonlinear observables.
elements.sent-2411-331: Furthermore, we also demonstrated that the estimators can be determined based only on experimentally accessible data and/or on knowledge of the unconditional dynamics, although in our bosonic model they can be calculated analytically.
elements.sent-2411-332: In the case of the lattice model, we also showed that the estimators are governed by a coherence length, which can be tuned to be much larger than the lattice spacing, in which case a continuum description becomes appropriate.
elements.sent-2411-333: At distances larger than the lattice spacing but shorter than the coherence length the estimators display power-law behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-334: We believe that these results open up many questions.
elements.sent-2411-335: To begin with, it is not clear whether this methodology can be extended to more complicated systems, where analytical solutions are no longer available.
elements.sent-2411-336: This could be analyzed by studying systems with increasing levels of complexity: gaussian bosons~\cite{Minoguchi2022}$\to$ few spins~\cite{lin2023}$\to$ dilute extended systems~\cite{Jin2024 , etc. The implementations described in Section~\ref{sec:ExpImp also suggest experimentally relevant generalizations, such as the inclusion of Kerr nonlinearities in ciruit-QED (leading to a Bose-Hubbard Hamiltonian~\cite{Ma2019 ) or the full nonlinear spin dynamics in cavity-QED.}}}
elements.sent-2411-337: We do not expect nonlinear observables in volume law phases to be captured by any simple estimation procedure, but it may be feasible to do so in area law phases, where measurements wash out the information, presumably leading to finite correlation times and lengths.
elements.sent-2411-338: The procedure outlined at the end of section~\ref{sec:SSite:FilterExperimental may be implementable in those cases, and an evaluation of its efficacy/efficiency is something that we defer to future work.}
